index.title: Helen Bao
index.content:
I am Helen Bao — Professor of Urban Economics and Public Policy in the Department of Land Economy, University of Cambridge. I serve as Deputy Head of Department, Director of the Lab for Interdisciplinary Spatial Analysis, Director of International Relations, and Academic Project Director for the School of Humanities and Social Sciences. I am a Fellow of Newnham College.
My research applies behavioural economics, field experiments, and computational methods to questions about housing markets, urban regulation, and the unequal distribution of urban opportunity. The questions cluster around three things I find interesting: how people actually make housing decisions (rather than how textbooks predict they will), how institutional and regulatory environments produce or correct unequal outcomes, and how new data sources let us measure both at the scale at which they actually operate.
Current research
Two major projects anchor my current work:

Housing discrimination and inequality — multi-country correspondence-test evidence on how landlords, agents, and platforms screen applicants by race, ethnicity, and foreignness, and the policy interventions that can shift these patterns.
Spatial personality — population-scale inference of psychological traits from digital traces, and how these traits aggregate to shape regional outcomes in entrepreneurship, wellbeing, voting, and resilience.

A signature line running for over a decade is the behavioural economics of housing decisions — reference dependence, loss aversion, and endowment effects applied to homebuyers, renters, and investors. A newer direction applies AI and computational methods to spatial-analytic questions that could not have been asked five years ago.
See the Research page for fuller descriptions, the Publications page for the full corpus, and the Working papers page for current preprints.
Recent

Under review at the Journal of Urban Economics (2026): “Race, symbolic stigma and foreignness: decomposing the housing-discrimination penalty”.
2025: Behavioural Economic Analysis of Institutional Changes: Lessons learned from China’s Land System Reforms (Routledge, with Lei Feng).
Active: The Lab for Interdisciplinary Spatial Analysis launched in 2024, bringing together researchers across Land Economy, Computer Science, and applied psychology.

Contact
Department of Land Economy, University of Cambridge
17 Mill Lane, Cambridge CB2 1RX, United Kingdom
Email: hxb20@cam.ac.uk
Phone: +44 (0)1223 337 116

research.title: Research
research.content:
Five interconnected research lines. Two are major active projects with multiple papers in flight; one is a signature line published continuously since 2010; the other two are an established China-focused theme and a newer methodological direction.
Housing discrimination and inequality
Major active project.
The housing-discrimination literature has long documented that landlords, agents, and increasingly digital platforms screen applicants by name, photograph, and biographical detail. What that screening signals — race, ethnicity, religion, foreignness — varies sharply across contexts, but is often lumped together as “ethnic-minority discrimination” in syntheses. My current work argues that this lumping conceals more than it reveals. A tripartite framework I propose separates race (deep-rooted, colonial-era hierarchies), symbolic stigma (event-triggered marking, e.g. Chinese applicants during COVID-19), and foreignness (citizenship-based screening with no necessary racial content). A meta-analysis of 54 correspondence-test studies across 22 countries shows that the discrimination penalty decomposes meaningfully into these three components, and that policy levers differ across them.
Related work examines how housing-regulation failures and crisis events shape the political accountability landscape (the Tai Po fire natural experiment is one current example), and how anti-discrimination policy interventions — warning letters, sanctions, customer-side norm-shifting — perform in randomised field-experimental designs.
Representative papers: Bao (2026, under review), Wang et al. (2026, JRFM), Huang & Bao (2026, Housing Studies).
Spatial personality
Major active project, emerging.
People differ on stable psychological traits — the Big Five framework is the dominant operationalisation — and these traits predict everything from career choices to political participation to health outcomes. Until recently, personality research has been constrained by self-report questionnaires that cannot scale beyond a few thousand participants. The advent of digital traces (social-media text, mobility data, visual content, smartphone sensors) and AI-based inference methods has shifted the constraint: we can now estimate personality at the scale of millions and aggregate the estimates to regional units (counties, cities, nations).
My current work introduces a Domain–Scope Framework for AI-based personality inference, maps the methodological landscape across linguistic, visual, audio, smartphone, physiological and multimodal data sources, and applies the resulting infrastructure to substantive questions: how regional personality shapes entrepreneurship, residential mobility, urban resilience, and voting. A meta-analysis of geographic personality research across 36 published studies — and a methodological review of 95 papers in AI-based personality inference — provides the empirical and analytical backbone.
Representative papers: Bao, Zhang & Bao (2026, working), Obschonka et al. (2020, Small Business Economics, cited extensively in the WP).
Behavioural economics of housing decisions
Signature published line.
Households making housing decisions deviate systematically from textbook-rational behaviour. They are reference-dependent (anchored to the price they paid, even when current prices have moved); they exhibit loss aversion (refusing to sell at perceived losses, distorting market liquidity); they over-extrapolate recent trends, mis-time entry and exit. The behavioural-economics framing carries direct implications for housing policy: nudge-style interventions can adjust margins where the canonical rational-actor model predicts no response.
My work in this line spans reference dependence in UK and Chinese housing markets, loss aversion among developers timing project sales, and the behavioural foundations of homeownership across generations.
Representative papers: Bao et al. (2023, Housing Studies); Bao (2020, Behavioural Science and Housing Decision Making, Routledge); Bao et al. (2021, Journal of Housing and the Built Environment).
Land use policy in China
Established theme, ongoing.
Land-use regulation in China differs structurally from most market economies: rural and urban land regimes operate under distinct legal frameworks, local governments are simultaneously regulators and beneficiaries of land development, and the resulting policy outcomes carry implications for inequality, environmental quality, and economic productivity that I find continuously interesting.
My published work in this line examines the return of state control over land markets, off-farm employment and land-use efficiency, local-government debt financing of infrastructure, behavioural land-use policy, and the political economy of rural homestead reforms. Land Use Policy is my home journal in this line; six papers have appeared there since 2020.
Representative papers: Bao et al. (2024, Land Use Policy); Bao et al. (2026, Land Use Policy — on hidden costs of policy uncertainty in industrial land development).
AI and computational methods for spatial analysis
New direction.
A through-line connecting the two major projects is methodological: each depends on data and inference techniques that were not available a decade ago. This research line treats those methods as substantive research objects in their own right — surveying their capabilities, identifying their failure modes, and proposing frameworks for their responsible deployment in spatial-analytic contexts.
Current threads include AI-based personality inference (foundational to the Spatial Personality project), computational analysis of crisis-time political discourse on social media (with applications to housing-regulation accountability), and AI-driven urban-resilience perception research.
Representative papers: Tian, Li, Bao et al. (2026, Sustainable Cities and Society); Bao, Zhang & Bao (2026, working — methodological synthesis).

Working with me
I supervise doctoral candidates on the above themes. Prospective students should consult the Cambridge Land Economy PhD programme page for admissions information and contact me directly to discuss fit before applying.
I welcome collaboration enquiries from researchers in adjacent fields — applied psychology, computer science, urban planning, behavioural public administration. The Lab for Interdisciplinary Spatial Analysis is the institutional home for such work.
For specific paper inquiries or media requests, email me.

working-papers/wp---colonial-graylined-f.filePath: working-papers/WP - Colonial graylined foreign discrimination rental.md
working-papers/wp---colonial-graylined-f.title: Colonial, graylined and foreign discrimination in rental housing: meta-regression evidence from fifty-four correspondence tests
working-papers/wp---colonial-graylined-f.content:
Colonial, graylined and foreign discrimination in rental housing
Citation (preprint): Bao, H. X. H. (2026). Colonial, graylined and foreign discrimination in rental housing: meta-regression evidence from fifty-four correspondence tests. Working paper (JUE submission, 16 May 2026).
Status: Working paper, single-authored, JUE submission

One-line summary
Proposes a tripartite classification of housing-discrimination targets (colonial / graylined / foreign), anchors each category in a distinct mixture of Becker / Phelps-Arrow / Akerlof-Kranton mechanisms, and provides a meta-regression identification test on 54 correspondence-test studies across 22 countries showing that each category responds to its theoretically-paired country-level fractionalisation moderator.
Abstract
Correspondence-test studies of housing discrimination have accumulated steadily over the past two decades and consistently document that minority-named, immigrant-named or religiously-marked applicants receive fewer responses to rental inquiries than otherwise-comparable majority applicants. The economic theory of discrimination distinguishes three canonical mechanisms underlying this pattern: taste-based (Becker, 1957), statistical (Phelps, 1972; Arrow, 1973) and identity-driven (Akerlof and Kranton, 2000). We propose that the published correspondence-test record can be organised into three analytical categories of discrimination targets — colonial, graylined and foreign — each theoretically anchored in a different mixture of the three economic mechanisms. We extend the cross-study sample to fifty-four correspondence-test studies across twenty-two countries with fieldwork between 2003 and 2022 and provide a meta-regression identification test of the three-category reading. Each category responds to its theoretically-paired country-level diversity index: the colonial-by-ethnic-fractionalisation, graylined-by-religious-fractionalisation, and foreign-by-linguistic-fractionalisation interactions are each positive, statistically significant, and consistent in sign with the underlying mechanism. The pattern is robust to a FAT-PET correction for publication selection. Recent fieldwork from Singapore, Kuala Lumpur and Malaysia is consistent with the identity-economics reading and shows that the same identity can carry the discriminator role in one jurisdiction and the target role in another.
Current status / next step

Target venue: Journal of Urban Economics (JUE submission)
Last revised: 16 May 2026
Next action: revise to add scope statements (see Pending revisions below)
Project folder: Project dashboard (created 2026-05-20) — contains MOC, Outline, and Notes & arguments for ongoing revision work

Key contribution(s)

Tripartite typology of housing-discrimination targets — colonial / graylined / foreign — each grounded in a distinct mixture of the three economic mechanisms (taste, statistical, identity)
Identification test via theoretically-predicted moderators: each category responds to its paired country-level fractionalisation index (ethnic / religious / linguistic)
54-study meta-regression with FAT-PET correction for publication selection
Discriminator-role / target-role reversibility: the same identity (e.g., Malay-Muslim) is a target in non-Muslim-majority countries and a discriminator in Malay-Muslim-majority Malaysia. Strong evidence for the identity-economics reading.
Methodological diagnostic: estimates of any single category require simultaneous inclusion of the other two; studies with only majority + colonial profiles absorb foreign and graylined components into the colonial estimate.

Method / data

54 unique correspondence-test studies, 22 countries, fieldwork 2003-2022
Updated sample beyond 2024 analysis (added 16 studies, +2 countries: Austria, Turkey)
Meta-regression: study-level odds ratios on tripartite indicators (foreign omitted as baseline), gender, year, ethnic / religious / linguistic fractionalisation, major-city indicator, country population, former-colonial-power host indicator, sample size, within-study design indicator, continent fixed effects
Standard errors clustered at paper level
FAT-PET correction for publication selection (robustness)
Three illustrative recent studies referenced but not in regression sample: Singapore (Bao & Tan), Kuala Lumpur (Bao et al), Wang et al 2026 Malaysia

Preliminary findings

Saturated specification: colonial coefficient ≈ −0.10 vs foreign baseline; graylined ≈ −0.06; male-applicant ≈ −0.11
Three predicted moderator interactions (colonial × ethnic-fractionalisation, graylined × religious-fractionalisation, foreign × linguistic-fractionalisation) all positive, statistically significant
Robust to FAT-PET correction
2024 to 2026 update: smaller magnitudes than 2024 sample — possibly reflects upward shift in colonial and graylined estimates as more recent / more legally-mature markets enter

Literature this builds on

Paper - Auspurg Schneck Hinz 2019 Closed doors everywhere meta-analysis — canonical ethnic-discrimination meta-analysis
Paper - Flage 2018 Ethnic gender discrimination rental housing meta-analysis — gender+ethnic meta
Paper - Akerlof Kranton 2000 Economics and Identity QJE — identity-economics theoretical foundation
Paper - Akerlof Kranton 2010 Identity Economics book
Paper - Wang et al 2026 The price of landlords’ racial and ethnic discrimination — triangulates Malaysian discriminator-role finding
Paper - Sugasawa & Harano 2026 Tokyo discrimination — illustrates foreign-vs-ethnic-minority inseparability

Connects to (my own work)

An Anatomy of Housing Discrimination — this WP IS the meta-regression foundation; Anatomy paper extends to mechanism-level taxonomy
WP - Whats in a Name Ethnic Discrimination Japan — Japan empirical work that contributes to foreign-category understanding
WP - Foreignness Ethnicity Housing Discrimination
US - 2024 - Hiding my first name — behavioural-intervention paper; informs implications

Topics

Discrimination taxonomy — taxonomy framework
Statistical discrimination
Identity economics

Open issues / pushback received






Pending revisions (scope statements)
Added 2026-05-19 after a framework-scoping discussion. Two scope additions to make the paper’s contribution sharper by being explicit about what the framework does and does NOT claim.
Revision 1 — Introduction (after the tripartite categories are introduced)
Add a one-paragraph scope statement establishing what the framework covers:

The tripartite classification developed here organises discrimination on group-membership axes anchored in ethnic, religious, and citizenship categories, signalled at the inquiry stage by names, accents, telephone country codes, and similar written cues. Discrimination on other axes — disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, age, and gender — operates through different mechanism mixtures, distinct cue types (body-marked, couple-structured, behavior-disclosed rather than name-signalled), and possibly different moderators. Within LGBTQ+ discrimination specifically, gay/lesbian and trans patterns differ empirically (e.g., Abbate et al. 2024 find no discrimination against gay-male couples in Latin America but strong discrimination against trans couples) and would each require separate framework analysis. The present paper retains gender as a covariate but does not develop a fourth category to capture it; gender discrimination interacts with the three categories rather than constituting a fourth alongside them.

Revision 2 — Section 2.2 (end of the section defining the three categories)
Add a paragraph clarifying the cue-type scope of the framework:

The framework treats group-membership signals that landlords can read from names, telephone country codes, accents, or written cues at the inquiry stage. Body-marked signals (disability disclosure, age, presented gender) and behavior-disclosed signals (sexual orientation revealed via couple structure, gender-identity disclosure) operate at a different point in the screening process — typically the viewing stage rather than the inquiry stage — and follow distinct theoretical paths. The three categories developed below should not be assumed to extend to these axes without separate analysis.

Why these revisions matter

Sharpens the contribution by negative claim — readers know exactly what the paper claims and what it does not, which strengthens peer-review reception.
Pre-empts seminar / referee question: “What about disability / LGBTQ+ / age?” — the scope statement answers this directly.
Creates citation hooks for future work: by explicitly identifying axes that don’t fit, the WP signals research opportunities to follow-on authors.
Preserves the 3×3×3 elegance — three categories × three economic mechanisms × three fractionalisation moderators. Trying to absorb disability or LGBTQ+ into this would dilute the mapping; scoping them out preserves it.

Empirical notes supporting the scope decision
Sexual orientation / gender identity evidence in the broader literature (from your library):

Paper - Gouveia Nilsson Berggren 2020 Religiosity discrimination same-sex couples Portugal — same-sex couples face discrimination in Portugal; counterintuitive negative relationship with parish religiosity (opposite of what graylined would predict if religiosity is the moderator)
Paper - Hellyer 2021 Same-sex rural urban USA — null result in US rural+urban
Paper - Abbate et al 2024 Discrimination against gay and transgender people Latin America — gay-male NULL, trans STRONG with statistical pattern (job signal closes gap); within-LGBTQ+ heterogeneity makes single-category treatment indefensible
Paper - Flage 2021 Same-sex couples meta-analysis — heterogeneous meta-finding

Disability evidence:

Paper - Flage Le Gallo 2023 Disability discrimination French rental field experiment — disability disclosure produces large discrimination; mental disability faces largest gap. Stigma is persistent and body-anchored, not symbolic-event-triggered (so doesn’t fit graylined’s event-trigger framing).

working-papers/wp---geographic-distribut.title: Geographic Distribution of Personality Traits: A Meta-Analysis of Methodologies, Findings, and Applications
working-papers/wp---geographic-distribut.content:
Geographic Distribution of Personality Traits: A Meta-Analysis of Methodologies, Findings, and Applications
Citation (preprint): Bao H., Cao Z., Guo Y., Liu N. X., Zhang Y., & Bao H. X. H. (in preparation 2026). Geographic Distribution of Personality Traits: A Meta-Analysis of Methodologies, Findings, and Applications. Working paper.
Status: Drafting — Draft 1 has Sections 1–4 in place; data collection complete (36 papers, ~120+ regressions, 571 trait-coefficient cells); literature deep-treatment in progress (5 of 36 done 2026-05-20).
PDF: (not yet)

One-line summary
A meta-analysis of 36 regression studies using Big Five personality as regressors to predict geographically-aggregated outcomes, introducing a novel relative-importance metric (within-study trait β ÷ mean β across all five traits) that sidesteps the standard standardisation problem when dependent-variable means are not reported. Organises findings along two analytical axes — domain (economic / political / social) × scope (individual / societal).
Abstract
(To be drafted)
Current status / next step

Target venue: TBD; primary candidate Psychological Bulletin; backup Perspectives on Psychological Science or European Journal of Personality. See project Notes & arguments.
Last revised: 2026-05-20 (project folder formalised; literature deep-treatment in progress)
Next action: complete deep-treatment of remaining 31 of 36 papers; verify Excel data against PDFs; finalise heterogeneity and publication-bias analyses; write introduction.
Project folder: Project dashboard (formalised 2026-05-20) — contains MOC, Outline, Notes & arguments, Draft 1 docx, and data/Paper summary 20250714.xlsx.

Key contribution(s)

Relative-importance metric: novel methodological contribution that enables meta-analysis of multi-coefficient regression studies (Big Five) when DV standardisation is infeasible.
Two-axis analytical framework (domain × scope) for organising trait-outcome research.
Cross-national synthesis of 36 studies covering economic, political, social outcomes across 30+ countries.
PCA of country-level relative-importance profiles revealing latent “agentic-individualism” and “cognitive-flexibility / affect-sensitivity” dimensions.
Anchors the broader Spatial Personality research line — application papers (WP - Residential Mobility Personality etc.) will cite this for the framework.

Method / data

36 papers, ~120+ regressions, 571 trait-coefficient cells.
Paper-level coding (15 fields per paper) and study-level coding (52 fields per regression-trait observation) in Paper summary 20250714.xlsx.
Relative-importance metric: trait β ÷ mean(β across 5 traits in same regression).
Two-axis classification: domain × scope.
Visualisations: boxplots, heatmaps, PCA biplot, trait-loading plot.

Preliminary findings

Extraversion + Conscientiousness dominant in economic and social domains, particularly individual-scope.
Openness elevated in political domain and societal scope.
Agreeableness + Neuroticism weaker across most cells.
Substantial cross-national variation — PCA shows systematic country clustering along latent dimensions.
Individual ≠ societal trait effects — partial misalignment between micro and macro effects.

Literature this builds on
(36 papers; deep-treated literature notes in 30 Resources/Literature/Spatial Personality/Notes/; 5 done as of 2026-05-20.)

Study 1 - Ebert - 2021 — economic individual; UK; prototypical spending
Study 2 - Avery - 2015 — political societal; USA; presidential vote share by state
Study 3 - Barceló - 2017 — political societal; 51 countries; regime type
Study 4 - Bleidorn - 2016 — social individual; like-minded clustering
Study 5 - Cuomo - 2024 — social societal; regional psychosocial characteristics
(31 more deep-treatments pending)

Connects to (my own work)

Mapping Personality at Scale AI — sister project; AI/ML personality-inference methodology; Section 4 overlap to be coordinated

working-papers/wp---urban-resilience-per.title: Physical recovery overestimates urban resilience by masking residents' perception dynamics
working-papers/wp---urban-resilience-per.content:
Physical recovery overestimates urban resilience by masking residents’ perception dynamics
Citation (preprint): Zhao, Chen, Bao, Zheng, Li, Taylor, Meng, & Fang (2026). Physical recovery overestimates urban resilience by masking residents’ perception dynamics. Working paper.
Status: Working paper

One-line summary
Synthesizes millions of geotagged citizen appeals with IoT infrastructure logs to quantify a “resilience perception gap” — the disconnect between physical infrastructure recovery and residents’ subjective lived experience after a disaster.
Abstract
Conventional resilience metrics prioritise physical infrastructure restoration on the assumption that technical service recovery equates to societal recovery. This study uses a catastrophic historic rainstorm as a natural experiment to quantify the resilience perception gap. By synthesizing millions of geotagged citizen appeals with real-time IoT infrastructure logs, the authors show the gap is structured (not noise) and driven by urbanisation inequalities and the hierarchy of human needs. Physical metrics overestimate resilience for survival necessities (30.2% lag) while underestimating livelihood needs (10.4% surplus).
Current status / next step

Target venue:
Last revised: 2026-01-02 (manuscript date)
Next action:
Project folder: Project dashboard (created 2026-05-20) — contains MOC, Outline, and Notes & arguments for ongoing work

Key contribution(s)

Establishes “resilience perception gap” as a structured, measurable phenomenon (not noise)
Demonstrates that physical-infrastructure-only resilience metrics systematically mis-state social recovery
Uses a novel data fusion: geotagged citizen appeals + IoT infrastructure logs

Method / data

Natural experiment: catastrophic historic rainstorm
Data: millions of geotagged citizen appeals + real-time IoT infrastructure logs
Machine learning used in Step 4 (per author note — verify when filling in)

Preliminary findings

30.2% lag: physical metrics overestimate resilience for survival necessities
10.4% surplus: physical metrics underestimate resilience for livelihood needs
Gap is structured by urbanisation inequalities and Maslow-style hierarchy of needs

Connects to (my own work)

JME - 2025 - Postdisaster infrastructure recovery — your earlier resilience framework paper, same first author (Zhao) — direct continuity
